← Back to Deep SummariesFull text ↗

zQR: A Verifiable QR-Driven zkSNARK Proof Verification Framework for Mobile Platforms

arXiv:2606.27092 [cs.CR] · June 2026 · full view ↗

Overview and motivation

This paper (single author, Goshgar C. Ismayilov, Boğaziçi University; arXiv 2606.27092, cs.CR, submitted 25 June 2026) proposes zQR, a proof-of-concept framework that encodes zkSNARK proofs inside QR codes so they can be verified offline on commodity smartphones. The central thesis is that zero-knowledge proofs (ZKPs) offer strong privacy but suffer from poor real-world adoption due to technical complexity, while QR codes are ubiquitous, hardware-free, offline-capable, and familiar. Bridging the two yields a "frictionless" interaction model for privacy-preserving but publicly verifiable and auditable interactions.

The work is framed around three motivation classes:

  • Privacy-driven — aligns with GDPR principles of data minimization (collect only what is needed, e.g. proving age > 18), selective disclosure (reveal a statement, not the underlying attribute), and privacy by design.
  • Accessibility-driven — prior privacy protocols (payments, healthcare, insurance, voting, supply chain) focus on theory and assume restrictive deployment conditions, widening the gap between theory and practice.
  • Literature-driven — no systematic, application-agnostic study of verifiable QR-driven zkSNARK verification on mobile exists; the absence of standardization hampers reproducibility and comparison.

Six engineering challenges are explicitly targeted: lightweightness, latency, scalability, privacy-preservation, low-effort (no ZKP expertise required of end users), and security.

The four claimed contributions are: (1) advancing the idea of verifiable QR codes; (2) a novel unified QR-driven zkSNARK framework integrating an LLM for low-code circuit generation and blockchain for on-chain verification; (3) an experimental study of temporal and financial costs plus a security discussion across three threat surfaces; and (4) a discussion of applications, limitations, and future directions.

Related work and positioning

Very few works combine QR codes with ZKPs. The paper contrasts zQR against four prior systems and summarizes the comparison in Table 1.

  • [10] (2025, zkSNARKs, supply chain) — QR codes are not inherently verifiable; they redirect to a server, so verification is not offline, and the circuit is application-specific and non-customizable.
  • [9] (2022, zkSNARKs, voting) — a polling officer scans and verifies proofs, but the scheme is application-specific with no architectural framework and no security discussion.
  • [28] (2024, Plonk + Verkle trees, generic) — the most closely related; a verifier scans attribute QR codes, with blockchain on-chain verification, but it is tightly coupled to a Verkle-tree anonymous-credential construction and lacks evaluation across circuit configurations.
  • [6] (2022, Hyperledger AnonCreds, healthcare) — transfers verifiable credentials via QR for vaccination passes; application-specific, no generalizable framework.

zQR is positioned as the only entry that is simultaneously dynamic (arbitrary, automatically generated circuits), LLM-assisted, blockchain-integrated, and application-agnostic (generic), with an informal security threat discussion. No prior work in the table offers a formal security analysis.

Framework architecture: roles and threat model

The framework defines three active roles (initiate actions) and two passive roles (respond on invocation):

  • Issuer (S) — constructs proof circuits (manually or via LLM); performs trusted setup to produce the proving key pk and verification key vk; deploys the proof-verifying smart contract.
  • Prover (P) — holds pk and sensitive data (e.g. age, health data); generates ZKPs locally; encodes proofs into verifiable QR codes; optionally submits proofs on-chain.
  • Verifier (V) — holds vk and public information only; decodes the QR code and verifies the proof offline.
  • Smart Contract (SC) — auto-generated by embedding vk into the ZoKrates contract template; deployed by the issuer; verifies proofs on-chain.
  • Large-Language Model (LLM) — receives a user prompt (proof specification + example inputs) plus a system prompt (instructions, boundaries, constraints) and returns a proof circuit.

The connections are issuer–LLM, issuer–blockchain, issuer–prover, issuer–verifier, prover–verifier, and prover–blockchain (Figure 1).

Heterogeneous role-based threat model:

  • Issuers and verifiers are honest-but-curious — they follow the rules but may seek extra sensitive information.
  • LLMs are honest-but-curious — they never intentionally manipulate responses, but outputs may be syntactically correct yet semantically inconsistent with issuer intent.
  • Provers are computationally bounded but potentially malicious — they cannot break standard zkSNARK assumptions but may manipulate inputs, construct invalid proofs, or delay actions.

Workflows: issuer, prover, verifier

Issuer workflow. The issuer builds a high-level proof program either manually (line-by-line, slower but finer control) or automatically (LLM prompt, lower technical barrier; the LLM acts as a semantic-to-arithmetic translator). The program is compiled into an arithmetic circuit by a ZKP compiler, after which a trusted setup produces $(\mathit{pk}, \mathit{vk}) \leftarrow \mathsf{ZKSetup}(1^{\lambda}, C)$. The proving key goes to prover devices and the verification key to verifier devices. Crucially, the issuer is assumed honest and must irreversibly destroy the common reference string (toxic waste) after key generation. Finally the issuer deploys the smart contract.

Prover workflow. The mobile interface collects public and private inputs; a local proof generator builds a witness and then a succinct proof $\pi \leftarrow \mathsf{ZKProve}(\mathit{pk}, w, x)$. The proof is rendered as a verifiable QR code on screen, and (optionally) the public inputs and proof are submitted on-chain. Two invariants: private inputs must never leave the device, and the device must have enough compute/memory for proof generation.

Verifier workflow. The verifier scans the QR code on the prover's screen, decodes it to recover $\pi$, and verifies $b \leftarrow \mathsf{ZKVerify}(\mathit{vk}, \pi, x)$ offline. Because verification keys are small and constant-sized and verification is near-constant-time, this is well suited to constrained devices. This three-party interaction is the variant zQR.v1.

zQR.v2 (lightweight variant). The prover workflow is eliminated entirely: the issuer also plays the prover, generating the QR-encoded proof from pk and chosen inputs. The verifier must hold the matching vk to verify. This yields verification-key-based access control — only holders of the correct vk can verify — whose security rests entirely on the confidentiality of vk; a single leaked key breaks access control for everyone. The two variants are formalized in Algorithm 1 and Algorithm 2 (see the Algorithms appendix).

Integrating QR codes, the LLM workflow, and the blockchain workflow

QR integration. The pipeline transforms a high-level proof program → arithmetic circuit → zero-knowledge proof → verifiable QR code (Figure 3). There is a direct relationship between proof size and QR symbol version: larger proofs require higher symbol versions and visually more complex codes. Minimizing proof size is therefore important for compact, user-friendly QR codes.

LLM workflow. zQR connects to an LLM via asynchronous HTTP for automatic circuit generation, lowering the barrier so issuers need no ZKP expertise. The issuer specifies the statement (public + private inputs); this is merged with a fixed system prompt and submitted to the external OpenAI API. From a privacy standpoint, no sensitive witness data is sent — only proof specifications — though the circuit structure itself may leak the application's nature. Because LLM output is not guaranteed semantically correct, human-in-the-loop validation is recommended before trusted setup. The workflow adds latency from LLM generation plus network transmission.

Blockchain workflow. zQR connects via a crypto-wallet (MetaMask) to an EVM-compatible chain. The issuer compiles and deploys the contract (a one-time cost the issuer bears); once deployed it is publicly accessible at its address, and verifiers incur no extra cost. On-chain verification calls the contract's verifyTx function with the public inputs and the proof. Because verifyTx carries the view modifier it is read-only and gas-free; however, if verifyTx is invoked indirectly by a state-changing function (e.g. transferring assets on successful verification), the prover must pay the corresponding gas. Any change to a circuit requires redeploying its contract, since each contract is bound to a circuit-unique verification key — a one-time additional cost.

Security threat discussion

The threat model assumes honest-but-curious issuers/verifiers and computationally-bounded malicious provers. Stronger settings (malicious issuers/verifiers, side-channel attacks) are out of scope. Threats are grouped into four surfaces:

Zero-knowledge proof threats.

  • Compromising toxic waste — if the common reference string from trusted setup leaks, soundness breaks and adversaries can forge proofs for false statements. Mitigation: an MPC setup ceremony where soundness holds if at least one node is honest (at the cost of coordination); current assumption is an honest issuer who destroys the toxic waste. Trustless alternatives such as zkSTARKs are noted as future work.
  • Proof replaying — non-interactive, publicly verifiable proofs can be re-scanned and re-verified; a valid proof (or a cloned/photographed QR code) lets an adversary impersonate a prover without knowing the witness. Mitigation: an on-chain registry of proof identifiers to detect replays.

QR code threats.

  • Over-the-shoulder attacks — private inputs typed on screen (the framework does not persist them) may be observed via shoulder-surfing, or exposed/altered on a compromised device (keyloggers). This cannot be fully eliminated at the framework level.
  • QR payload injection — malformed proof payloads may exploit decoding-library vulnerabilities; mitigation is strict proof-format validation before verification.

Blockchain threats.

  • Transaction replaying — replaying proof-carrying transactions to re-trigger verification; mitigation is per-transaction nonces, though front-running (resubmitting with higher gas) remains possible.
  • Smart contract attacks — ZoKrates template contracts may be vulnerable to re-entrancy, integer overflow/underflow; security is bounded by ZoKrates' guarantees.

Operational threats.

  • Denial-of-service — flooding blockchain interactions or the LLM endpoint; mitigation via rate limiting, resource monitoring, and early-detection alarms.
  • Prompt manipulation — a malicious prompt to the LLM can yield circuits that compile but fail to enforce the intended semantics; mitigation is human-in-the-loop validation before trusted setup.

Experimental setup and circuit specifications

Experiments run on three platforms spanning a wide capability range: zQR-macOS (MacBook Air, Apple M2, 8 GB RAM, 8 cores), zQR-iOS (iPhone 15, A16 Bionic, 6 GB RAM, 6 cores), and zQR-aOS (Samsung A2, quad-core, 2 GB RAM, 4 cores). The interfaces use zokrates.js for proof generation/verification and qrcode.js for QR encode/decode. The LLM workflow uses OpenAI GPT-4.1, connected over HTTP. The blockchain workflow uses Solidity contracts deployed to the Ethereum Sepolia testnet via MetaMask.

Eight circuits of increasing complexity are evaluated (Table 2). C1 is the simplest (prove a private value squared equals a public value, no crypto ops); C8 is the most complex (prove the public root hash of an 8-leaf Merkle tree computed via 7 SHA-256 operations). Note the proving key grows from 2 KB to 138.5 MB while the verification key and proof size stay essentially constant.

CircuitPrivate inputsPublic inputsConstraintsProving keyVerification keyProof size
C11122 KB2 KB1 KB
C2123,3091.3 MB2 KB1 KB
C32448,88719.7 MB2 KB1 KB
C43652,19620.8 MB3 KB1 KB
C58497,77339.5 MB2 KB1 KB
C686145,19962.9 MB3 KB1 KB
C782232,30990.6 MB2 KB1 KB
C882330,077138.5 MB2 KB1 KB

Results: temporal costs (proof generation, verification, QR encode/decode)

Observation 1 — proof generation grows with circuit complexity and is the main bottleneck. Proof generation time rises with the number of arithmetic constraints. On macOS, C1 (2 constraints) takes about $0.35$ s, whereas C7 ($232{,}309$ constraints) takes about $103.6$ s. The aOS device is much slower even on small circuits. Due to memory limits, aOS, iOS, and macOS cannot generate proofs for C3, C6, and C8 respectively. On iOS, circuits up to roughly $100{,}000$ constraints finish within a practically acceptable $\sim 20$ s. Mitigations cited: vertical batching (split the program into sub-programs proven sequentially to lower peak load), zkSNARK-friendly hashes such as Poseidon (drastically smaller circuits), and outsourcing proof generation to external APIs.

Observation 2 — off-chain verification is near-constant-time. Verification is far cheaper than generation and roughly independent of circuit size. macOS and iOS fluctuate between about $0.3$ and $0.5$ s; aOS is higher at about $4.2$ to $4.7$ s but still acceptable. This makes the lightweight zQR.v2 variant (which drops generation and keeps only constant-time verification) especially suited to constrained devices.

Observation 3 — QR encoding/decoding times are constant. Both operations are stable across all circuits, a direct consequence of the constant zkSNARK proof size. Encoding stays below $0.05$ s on macOS/iOS and about $0.32$ to $0.38$ s on aOS. (Environmental factors such as lighting, focus, and motion blur are excluded.)

Observation 4 — QR encode/decode is feasible on all platforms. Decoding (measured from successful camera detection) stays within $0.02$ to $0.03$ s on macOS/iOS and $0.28$ to $0.33$ s on aOS; even on the weakest device the combined QR overhead is below one second.

Observation 5 — QR symbol version varies with error-correction level for a fixed proof size. For the constant proof size, encoding requires symbol version 19 (low error correction), 22 (medium), 27 (quartile), and 32 (high). Higher correction reserves more redundancy and reduces payload capacity. The low level gives the most compact code and is recommended for screen-to-camera scanning; higher levels suit printed surfaces subject to damage.

Results: financial costs and circuit complexity

Observation 6 — deployment cost grows linearly with public inputs. Since proof size is constant, the dominant factor in contract-deployment gas is the number of public inputs, rising at roughly $30{,}000$ gas units per input. Using $1.5$ Gwei per gas unit and $2{,}023$ USD per ETH, deployment with two public inputs costs about $0.00171$ ETH ($\approx 3.45$ USD) and with ten public inputs about $0.00208$ ETH ($\approx 4.20$ USD).

Observation 7 — on-chain verification is free for provers when called directly, because ZoKrates declares the verifier with the view modifier (read-only):

function verifyTx(Proof memory proof, uint[n] memory input) public view returns (bool r)

If verifyTx is invoked indirectly by a state-changing function, the associated gas must be paid.

Observation 8 — constraint count and proving-key size grow with computational complexity. Cryptographic operations (hashing, public-key encryption) expand the arithmetic circuit. C1 (square check, no crypto) yields 2 constraints and a 2 KB proving key; C8 (8-leaf Merkle root via 7 SHA-256 ops) yields $330{,}077$ constraints and a $138.5$ MB proving key. Large proving keys can exceed device memory and cause generation to fail.

Observation 9 — verification-key size and proof size stay constant across all circuits. Even as constraints grow from 2 to $330{,}077$, vk stays in a narrow range and the proof size is fixed. Constant vk matters because ZoKrates embeds it in the contract (so deployment gas does not balloon), and constant proof size makes QR symbol-version selection uniform across all circuits.

Applications, limitations, and future directions

Applications. The author sketches five privacy-sensitive domains: physical access control (prove credentials/attributes such as being over 18 or present on campus without revealing identity), payment and purchase (anonymous purchasing and private tax calculation without disclosing financial detail), anonymous membership and subscription (and event ticketing), anonymous voting (polling stations challenge voters to prove eligibility and non-participation), and border control / anonymous traveling (prove compliance via a QR scan instead of disclosing passports, biometrics, and travel history).

Limitations and future directions.

  • Trusted issuer workflow — zkSNARK soundness depends on destroying the common reference string; ZoKrates partially addresses this via a multi-party ceremony. Trusted-setup-free alternatives (zkSTARKs) are promising but produce larger proofs that may exceed QR symbol-version capacity.
  • Stronger threat model — the PoC assumes honest-but-curious issuers/verifiers; real settings may need malicious-issuer/verifier and side-channel (e.g. timing) considerations.
  • Proof size and symbol versions — fixed succinct proofs fit in symbol version 19, but smaller payloads (alternative serialization, more compact elliptic-curve representations) would yield friendlier codes.
  • Stronger blockchain integration — currently EVM-only; extending to non-EVM and multi-chain ecosystems is challenging (gas, libraries, cross-chain bridging).
  • Better LLM performance — faster, more semantically accurate models would improve automatic circuit generation; prior work [16] shows LLM-based generation feasible up to about $200{,}000$ constraints.
  • Scalability — deployments, on-chain accounts, network throughput, and external LLM latency all raise scalability concerns for production.

Conclusion. zQR demonstrates the feasibility of QR-encoded zkSNARK proofs for offline mobile verification, with two variants (v1 three-party, v2 lightweight issuer-as-prover). Proof generation is the main bottleneck (up to minutes for complex circuits); verification is cheap and gas-free for provers, while deployment is a modest one-time issuer cost. The author argues QR codes can be a key enabling technology for broader ZKP adoption.

Algorithms

The paper formalizes its two operating variants in Algorithm 1 (zQR.v1, full three-party interaction) and Algorithm 2 (zQR.v2, lightweight issuer-as-prover). Notation: $C$ is the proof circuit, $u$ the user prompt, $x$ the public inputs, $w$ the witness (private inputs), $(\mathit{pk}, \mathit{vk})$ the proving/verification key pair, $1^{\lambda}$ the security parameter, $\pi$ the proof, $\mathit{qr}_{\pi}$ the QR-encoded proof, and $b \in \{0,1\}$ the verification result. Both algorithms are reproduced verbatim below.

Algorithm 1: zQR.v1 Framework

1. Setup:
   1.1  Issuer manually constructs the proof circuit, C.
   1.2  Prompt-LLM:
        1.2.1  Issuer prompts LLM to generate circuit,  C  <- S.LLM(u, x).
   1.3  Issuer performs trusted setup,                 (pk, vk) <- S.ZKSetup(1^lambda, C).
   1.4  Deploy-OnChain:
        1.4.1  Issuer deploys verification key to chain, SC(addr) <- S.Deploy(vk).

2. Generate:
   2.1  Prover accesses proving key pk and prepares witness w and public inputs x.
   2.2  Prover generates a proof,                       pi  <- P.ZKProve(pk, w, x).
   2.3  Prover encodes proof to QR code,                qr_pi <- QREncode(pi).
   2.4  Verify-OnChain:
        2.4.1  Prover submits proof to contract,        b   <- V.ZKVerify(addr, pi, x).

3. Verify:
   3.1  Verifier accesses QR code and decodes it,       pi  <- QRDecode(qr_pi).
   3.2  Verifier verifies the proof,                    b   <- V.ZKVerify(vk, pi, x).
Algorithm 2: zQR.v2 Framework  (lightweight: prover workflow removed)

1. Setup:
   1.1  Issuer manually constructs the proof circuit, C.
   1.2  Prompt-LLM:
        1.2.1  Issuer prompts LLM to generate circuit,  C  <- S.LLM(u, x).
   1.3  Issuer performs trusted setup,                 (pk, vk) <- S.ZKSetup(1^lambda, C).
   1.4  Issuer generates a verifiable proof,           pi  <- S.ZKProve(pk, w, x).
   1.5  Issuer encodes proof to QR code,               qr_pi <- QREncode(pi).

2. Verify:
   2.1  Verifier accesses QR code and decodes it,      pi  <- QRDecode(qr_pi).
   2.2  Verifier verifies the proof iff correct vk present,
                                                       b   <- V.ZKVerify(vk, pi, x).

The smart-contract verification routine referenced by the on-chain path is the ZoKrates-generated, read-only (gas-free) function:

function verifyTx(Proof memory proof, uint[n] memory input) public view returns (bool r)
